coredata.dc:description: Widespread bacterial infection and the emergence of antibiotic resistance exhibit an increasing threat to public health. Additionally, chronic wounds caused by bacterial infection have become a major challenge and threat in medical. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore effective and safe nanomaterials which possess antibacterial and wound healing promotion performance. Herein, we developed silica-supported near-infrared carbon dots (QPCuRC@MSiO2) and bicarbonate (BC) nanoplatform (BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA), which possess triple synergistic antibacterial including quaternary ammonium compounds (QACs), photothermal therapy (PTT), and photodynamic therapy (PDT). Meanwhile, the nanoplatform realized the controlled release of CO2 in situ triggered by 808 nm laser irradiation for wound healing. In vitro and in vivo antibacterial assays displayed that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA possess excellent antibacterial property, the antibacterial rate up to 99.6% and 99.99% to Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), respectively. Wound healing evaluation proved that suitable release of CO2 could promote the process of infected wound healing, and the wound healing rate up to 100% after treatment for 14 days. Additionally, the cellular imaging experiment revealed that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA could be considered as fluorescence probe. Together, these results demonstrated that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA have great potential in biomedical field.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Widespread bacterial infection and the emergence of antibiotic resistance exhibit an increasing threat to public health. Additionally, chronic wounds caused by bacterial infection have become a major challenge and threat in medical. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore effective and safe nanomaterials which possess antibacterial and wound healing promotion performance. Herein, we developed silica-supported near-infrared carbon dots (QPCuRC@MSiO2) and bicarbonate (BC) nanoplatform (BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA), which possess triple synergistic antibacterial including quaternary ammonium compounds (QACs), photothermal therapy (PTT), and photodynamic therapy (PDT). Meanwhile, the nanoplatform realized the controlled release of CO2 in situ triggered by 808 nm laser irradiation for wound healing. In vitro and in vivo antibacterial assays displayed that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA possess excellent antibacterial property, the antibacterial rate up to 99.6% and 99.99% to Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), respectively. Wound healing evaluation proved that suitable release of CO2 could promote the process of infected wound healing, and the wound healing rate up to 100% after treatment for 14 days. Additionally, the cellular imaging experiment revealed that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA could be considered as fluorescence probe. Together, these results demonstrated that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA have great potential in biomedical field.

ce:section.ce:para: Infectious diseases caused by bacteria with high morbidity and mortality threaten the health of human [1–4], which extremely difficult to cure and afflict millions of people every year [5]. Skin is the largest organ, and it plays an essential role in preventing bacterial invasion and protecting human body [6]. Therefore, it is crucial to accelerate the healing process of defective skin and prevent bacterial infection. The traditional strategies depend on antibiotics therapy, which could effectively decrease the pain of patients. However, the strategy of antibiotics therapy suffers the drawbacks of weakened efficacy due to the development of bacterial resistance [7,8]. In particular, the healing of chronic wounds and trauma, which emerged in surgery, is a major challenge in the healthcare industry. Therefore, developing a novel antibacterial agent is urgently needed to treat bacterial infection and accelerate wound repair. Over the past few years, many antibacterial materials have emerged, including quaternary ammonium salts [9–11], cationic polymers [12,13], antibacterial peptides [14,15], and inorganic nanoparticles (NPs) [16,17]. However, the effective applications were limited due to the easy aggregation of small nanoparticles and the development of drug resistance. Thus, it is urgent to design an effective therapeutic strategy to apply in antibacterial and wound healing.

Gas therapy, including nitric oxide (NO) [6,18], hydrogen sulfide (H2S) [19–21], oxygen (O2) [22,23], carbon dioxide (CO2) [24], has been proved promising in accelerating wound healing due to their excellent biocompatibility and high therapeutic efficiency. Thereby, gas therapy has attracted widespread attention. Compared to other therapy, gas therapy will not produce drug resistance and side effects to normal tissues because of gaseous molecules, which act as vital endogenous signaling molecules, play a key role in various biological processes. As displayed in previous reports, adequate oxygen supply could promote wound growth and remodeling. Therefore, hypoxia, which caused by insufficient microcirculation, is unfavorable for the healing of wound [25–27]. To facilitate the wound healing process, it is necessary to increase the O2 content around the wound. Currently, the traditional treatment strategy relies on delivering pure O2 to the wound or inhaling high concentrations of O2 [28]. However, the potential risk of oxygen poisoning, professional operation, and expensive equipment of oxygen therapies limited the application in biomedical field. According to the Bohr effect principle, CO2 could also be used to accelerate wound healing process [24,29,30]. The Bohr effect describes that the decrease of blood pH could enhance the release of oxygen and satisfy the oxygen needs of the tissue, which is a physiological phenomenon. The dissolution of CO2 in wound tissue could cause the decrease of pH, which would trigger more oxygen release from hemoglobin and effectively promote the healing process of infected wounds. Compared with oxygen therapy, the strategy of CO2 therapy displays more conveniently on the wound due to the high solubility in body fluid. To avoid the potential risk of CO2 therapy, including asphyxia and anoxia, developing a material with on-demand release of CO2 is urgently needed. Therefore, we anticipate designing a stable and biocompatible system with on-demand release of CO2 at the wound tissues.

Up to date, photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) have attracted widespread attention because of specific properties, including noninvasiveness, no drug resistance, deep tissue penetration, and remote controllability, which could accelerate tissue regeneration and wound healing [16,31,32]. A rapid increase in temperature would destroy bacterial membranes and proteins after the photothermal materials were irradiated under a suitable wavelength, leading to the death of bacteria. Recently, various NPs with excellent photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE, η) have been designed and applied in antibacterial field, such as gold nanocages [33], MoS2 nanosheets [34,35], carbon nanomaterials [36,37], and black phosphorus [38,39], which could overcome antibiotic resistance and realize effective sterilization. Additionally, polydopamine (PDA) has also attracted widespread attention due to its high adhesion, high photothermal conversion efficiency (40%), excellent biocompatibility, super biodegradability, and easy surface decoration with other functional components [40–43]. However, single-mode photothermal therapy generally requires high temperature (greater than70 ℃) to treat bacterial infection [44], which may cause the damage and inflammation of nearby healthy tissues. Previous researches also displayed that photo-sensitive materials could be triggered after irradiation, leading to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), including hydroxyl radical (·OH), single oxygen (1O2), and superoxide radical (·O2 –), which could destroy the bacterial membranes, DNA, and proteins, realizing effective sterilization [45,46]. Single-mode photodynamic therapy needs numerous ROS to kill microorganisms, and the excessive ROS may cause normal tissues inflammation and necrosis. Additionally, quaternary ammonium compounds (QACs) are considered as effective antibacterial materials all the time owing to their hydrophobic chains and positive charges [10,11]. The integration of multiple antibacterial strategies into one could shorten the antibacterial time, reduce the dose of antibacterial agents, and improve antibacterial efficiency. Thus, designing and developing a multi-mode synergistic antibacterial agent is urgently required for treating bacterial infection and promoting wound healing.

Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely applied in biomedical field, including biochemical detection, biological imaging, drug delivery, optical device, and biotherapy, due to their excellent biocompatibility, chemical stability, and super optical properties [47]. However, most of the reported CDs possess feature absorption in the ultraviolet (UV) or visible region, and only a few CDs displayed NIR absorption, which limited their applications in biomedical field. In our previous study, we have successfully developed novel copper doped NIR-emitting carbon dots, which exhibit a broad peak in the NIR area [31]. The composite displayed high photothermal conversion efficiency, excellent biocompatibility, broad-spectrum antibacterial property, even wound healing promotion. However, the size of the composite is too small and prefer to aggregate in solution, which may decrease its antibacterial performance to a certain degree. Previous reports revealed that silica sphere could be considered as a potential inorganic drug carrier owing to its super drug loading capability, tunable size, easy surface modifiability, low cytotoxicity, and excellent stability in vivo [2,48,49]. Furthermore, surface modification could impart new properties and functions to the material, such as hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity, roughness, surface charge properties, adhesion, and optical properties, while retaining their original bulk properties [50].

Based on the above considerations, for the first time, we reported silica-supported near-infrared carbon dots and bicarbonate (BC) nanoplatform, which combined phototherapy and gas therapy, for synergistic sterilization, bioimaging, and wound healing promotion. To improve the solubility of Cu-RCDs-C35, PEG 4000 was utilized for the passivation, and the compound was named as PEG-Cu-RCDs-C35 (QPCuRC) [51]. The branched poly-ethylenimine (BPEI) modified SiO2 sphere (MSiO2) plays a crucial role in preventing the aggregation of QPCuRC by regulating the size. The MSiO2 was combined with QPCuRC through electrostatic adsorption, and the compound was named as QPCuRC@MSiO2. Subsequently, the –NH3 +/N+ from QPCuRC@MSiO2 was used for loading bicarbonate to obtain BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2, which could produce CO2 when the temperature up to 42 ℃ [52,53]. In order to regulate the precise release of CO2 at the infective wound area, PDA shell was used to coat on the surface of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2, then obtained the antibacterial agent (BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA). The developed nanocomposite displayed a controlled release behavior of CO2 due to PDA has a special pH sensitivity and could degrade in acidic environment (pH = 5.5 ∼ 6.5) [54–56]. Additionally, the PDA coating could also enhance the photothermal effect of the composite, thereby improving the antimicrobial performance. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , we designed silica-supported near-infrared carbon dots and bicarbonate nanoplatform using solvothermal method and electrostatic interaction. Compared with other antibacterial materials, BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA exhibited fascinating advantages, including that: (1) the composite combined PTT, PDT, quaternary ammonium, and gas therapy to fight bacterial infection and promote wound healing at a low concentration; (2) the release of CO2 could be precisely regulated in the wound infection site; (3) the composite possesses higher photothermal conversion efficiency due to the synergy between QPCuRC and PDA coating; (4) the antibacterial agent could target the wound area, thereby reducing the phototoxicity to normal tissues; (5) the catechol groups of PDA and the enhanced roughness after combination of SiO2 and QPCuRC could improve the affinity between composite and bacteria; (6) the composite also possesses the potential in bioimaging field because of inherent luminescence. In summary, this research affords multifunctional silica-supported near-infrared carbon dots and bicarbonate nanoplatform for fighting bacterial infection and accelerating wound healing by combining phototherapy and gas therapy. Thereby, the nanoplatform could be expected to apply in biomedical field as a promising therapeutic strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PEG-Cu-RCDs (PCuRC) and QPCuRC NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The PCuRC NPs were synthesized by one-step solvothermal method. In short, the raw materials (RM) of 1 g PEG 4000, 1 g citric, 3 g urea, and 0.25 g CuCl2·2H2O were added into 20 mL DMSO and stirred until the powder was dissolved at 40 ℃. After that, the clear mixture was transferred into 50 mL Teflon autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 6 h. After cooling, a dark solution was obtained and filtered using a Millipore filter (0.22 µm) to remove the precipitate. Then, the filtrate was mixed with triple the volume of ethanol solution and then centrifuged to remove residual solvents and organic molecular byproducts (11000 rpm, 15 min). At last, the precipitate was harvested after being washed thrice with ethanol and deionized water, then resuspended in deionized water and dialyzed for 3 days with a cellulose dialysis bag. The water was replaced every 8 h. After freeze-dried, the pure PCuRC NPs were obtained, then stored at 4 ℃ for further use. The QPCuRC NPs were synthesized toward the procedure described in ref.[31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of SiO2 and MSiO2 sphere
ce:section.ce:para: SiO2 and MSiO2 spheres were prepared according to the previously described procedure. Briefly, 13 mL of ammonia (25%-28%), 63.3 mL of isopropanol, 23.5 mL deionized water were added into the flask and heated into 35 ℃ in an oil bath. Then, 0.6 mL of TEOS was added into the mixture dropwise and kept at 35 ℃ for 30 min. Next, 5 mL of TEOS was introduced into the above mixture dropwise and reacted for another 2 h. After centrifugation, repeated washing with ethanol and deionized water, the pure SiO2 sphere was obtained after freeze-dried, then stored at drying tank for further use. The preparation process of MSiO2 sphere was described as follows: 4 mL BPEI solution (86 mg/mL) was added into 200 mL ethanol with SiO2 sphere (2 mg/mL). Then, the mixtures were reacted and stirred at 60 ℃ for 4 h. After repeated washing with ethanol and deionized water, the MSiO2 sphere was harvested, and finally dried in air.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of QPCuRC@MSiO2, BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2, and BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA NPs
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesis the QPCuRC@MSiO2 NPs, 0.03 g positively charged MSiO2 was dispersed in 30 mL deionized water, then 5 mL negatively charged QPCuRC (0.2 mg/mL) was added into dispersions dropwise and stirred for 1 h. After centrifugation, the QPCuRC@MSiO2 NPs were repeated washing with ethanol and deionized water, then dried in air. The preparation process of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2 was described as follows: 7 mg QPCuRC@MSiO2 was dispersed in 1 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 30 min to form homogeneous solution. Next, 1 mL of ammonium bicarbonate aqueous solution with the concentration of 5 mM was added dropwise into QPCuRC@MSiO2 dispersion under ice bath (drip once 1 h, 4 times in total), then kept for 1 h. After that, the obtained BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2 NPs were centrifuged and washed with 0.5 mM ammonium bicarbonate solution for three times. Next, the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2 NPs were dispersed in 20 mL Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.8), which contained dopamine hydrochloride (20 mg), then slowly stirred for 4 h at room temperature. The final product was centrifuged and washed with deionized water for three times. After freeze-dried, the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA NPs were obtained, then stored at 4 ℃ for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitation of CO2 released from the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA
ce:section.ce:para: A certain amount of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA was dissolved in different pH aqueous solutions (pH = 5.8, 7.2) to form various concentrations solution (15, 30, 60, 120, and 240 µg/mL), respectively. The prepared solutions were centrifuged and added into excessive calcium hydroxide aqueous solution, respectively. Then these mixtures were irradiated for 10 min under 808 nm laser in a sealed container. Next, these mixtures were centrifuged after cooling to room temperature, the residual calcium hydroxide was removed, and the precipitations were collected. Afterwards, the precipitations were washed with deionized water for three times and then dissolved in 5% nitric acid solution. The content of Ca element was evaluated by ICP-AES, which could reflect the level of CO2 generation.

In vitro photothermal properties of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA were evaluated under 808 nm laser irradiation. For concentration-dependency of the photothermal performance evaluation, various doses of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA solution (0, 15, 30, 60, 120, 240 µg/mL) were irradiated for 10 min (808 nm, 2 W/cm2). Infrared thermal imaging camera (FLIR ONE Pro, USA) was used to record the thermal photos and real-time temperatures. The power-dependency of the photothermal performance of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA (240 µg/mL) was evaluated under different laser power densities (1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 W/cm2). Additionally, the photothermal stability of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA was also determined through irradiating and naturally cooling for six repeated cycles. The photothermal conversion efficiency was measured via monitoring the temperature changes of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA (240 µg/mL) in cooling period, and the calculation process of η was accorded to previous literature [24]. Additionally, to prove the modification of PDA could enhance the photothermal effect, the PCE of QPCuRC@MSiO2 was also evaluated and the procedure was the same as the evaluation of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA.

The generation of single oxygen was detected by electron spin resonance (ESR) and probe including 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) and 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) [45,57]. For ESR measurement, the glass capillary tubes, which contained the irradiated and non-irradiated BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA, were inserted into the ESR cavity and the ESR signals were recorded. The spin trap was used 2, 2, 6, 6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) to verify the generation of 1O2. For DPBF probe detection, the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA were dissolved in EtOH with a concentration of 240 µg/mL and then blended with 2 mM of DPBF solution (40 µL). After being continuous irradiated with 808 nm laser (2.0 W/cm2), the absorbance of DPBF (410 nm) was detected every 5 min, and the non-irradiation group was considered as a control. The intracellular ROS generation was detected using the probe of DCFH-DA (λex = 488 nm, λem = 525 nm). In short, 10 µM DCFH-DA solution was mixed with bacterial suspension including Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), then co-cultured for 20 min at 37 ℃, respectively. These mixtures were inverted every 3 min, then centrifuged and resuspended into BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA solution (240 µg/mL). Following incubation for 30 min in the dark, the mixture was exposed to 808 nm laser for 10 min (2.0 W/cm2), and the non-irradiation group was considered as a control. After that, these mixtures were co-cultured for another 2 h. Then the fluorescence intensity of DCFH-DA was determined at an excitation wavelength of 488 nm, which reflected the level of ROS generation.

The antibacterial performance of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA against E. coli and S. aureus was evaluated by plate counting method, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) imaging, and live/dead bacterial cells staining assay. The number of colony-forming units (CFUs) was used to calculate the antibacterial ratio of the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA. The process was mainly as follows: E. coli and S. aureus were diluted until the O.D.600 reached 0.1, then cultured with different concentrations of QPCuRC@MSiO2 and BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA (0, 15, 30, 60, 120, and 240 µg/mL), respectively. After being co-cultured for 2 h, these mixtures were irradiated with 808 nm laser (2 W/cm2, 10 min), then incubated for another 22 h under 120 rpm rotation at 37 ℃. The non-irradiation groups were considered as control groups. Next, 100 µL of the bacterial suspension was diluted to 105 times, then 30 µL diluted bacterial solution was spread on agar culture plates and incubated for 24 h in incubator until bacteria form colonies. Then the colonies on the agar plates were counted and the antibacterial rate was calculated in terms of the following formula:
Antibacterial rate = (CFUcg − CFUeg)/CFUcg × 100%
where CFUcg and CFUeg represent the number of colonies formed in control group and experimental group, respectively.

Additionally, the morphologies of E. coli and S. aureus were observed under SEM after being treated with different materials. The detailed process was as follows: E. coli and S. aureus were co-cultured with PBS, QPCuRC@MSiO2, and BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA for 2 h and exposed to 808 nm laser for 10 min with the power density of 2 W/cm2, then incubated for another 14 h. The non-irradiation groups were cultivated under the same conditions as irradiation groups, except irradiating with 808 nm laser. Afterwards, the bacterial cells were collected after centrifugation and washing with PBS for three times. Next, 2.5% glutaraldehyde was added and fixed for 4 h at 4 ℃ under dark conditions, then washed twice with PBS. After that, all samples were dehydrated with a series of ethanol/water mixtures (30, 50, 70, 90, and 100 vol%) for 15 min each time. Finally, the bacteria cells were resuspended in deionized water, then, taking 20 µL of the suspension to drop on silicon wafer. After perfectly drying at room temperature, these samples were treated with platinum at 30 mA for 1 min and then examined by SEM.

The live/dead bacterial cells staining assay was performed to assess the viability and membrane integrity of bacterial cells after being treated with different materials. Hoechst 33,342 (100 µg/mL) and propidium iodide (PI, 100 µg/mL) were utilized to quantify the dead bacteria and surviving bacteria via fluorescence photographs. Hoechst 33,342 could penetrate the bacterial cell membrane and emit blue fluorescence, while PI could only penetrate apoptotic bacteria through binding to nucleic acids and emit red fluorescence. The process of the assay was as follows: E. coli and S. aureus were cultured with PBS, QPCuRC@MSiO2, and BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA for 2 h, respectively. Then, these mixtures were irradiated with 808 nm laser (10 min, 2 W/cm2) and incubated for another 4 h. The non-irradiation groups were regarded as control groups. Next, the bacterial cells were collected after being centrifugated and washed twice with PBS. After that, the Hoechst 33,342 was added and incubated for 10 min, then centrifuged and washed with PBS. Afterwards, the PI was mixed with bacteria and incubated for another 10 min, centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min, washed and resuspended with PBS. 10 µL of the stained bacteria was dropped on glass slide and fixed with coverslip, then observed under confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antibacterial studies and wound healing evaluation
ce:section.ce:para:
The animal experiments were performed according to the guidelines of the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of China. Female Balb/c mice (6 weeks; 18–20 g) were used with the approval of the animal ethics committee of Nanjing Normal University. These mice were anaesthetized by 10% chloral hydrate solution (0.3 mL/100 g) via intraperitoneal injection. Next, depilatory cream was painted on the back of mice to remove the dorsal hair, and wounds with a diameter of 0.8 cm on the left-hand sides of their backbones were created. Subsequently, each wound was inoculated with 100 µL S. aureus (O.D.600 = 0.1) and wrapped with sterile nonwoven to construct the wound-infection model. After 1 day, all the mice were randomly divided into four groups, including Control + L group, QPCuRC@MSiO2 + L group, QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA + L group, and BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA + L group. For the Control + L group, 100 µL of PBS was dripped on the wounds of mice and irradiated with 808 nm NIR laser for 5 min, then fastened with sterile nonwoven. For the other groups, QPCuRC@MSiO2, QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA and BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA were dispersed in PBS with concentration of 240 µg/mL, then, taking 100 µL of these solutions to drop on the infected wounds and exposed to 808 nm NIR laser for 5 min, then covered with sterile nonwoven, respectively. The sterile nonwoven was changed every 2 days for 14 days and the wounds of these mice were recorded at 0, 1, 3, 7, 10, and 14 days. In addition, to quantitatively analyze the effect of antibacterial materials on the wound site of mice, the plate counting method was utilized. Sterile cotton swab was used to wipe the infected wound and stirred in a sterile centrifuge which containing sterile PBS (2 mL). Afterwards, these bacterial solutions were diluted to 103 times, then, taking 100 µL to spread on the agar plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 ℃. Additionally, to reflect the healing process and speed of the infected wound, the wound healing rate at different times was also calculated and the calculation formula was as follows:
Wound healing rate = 1 − St/S0 × 100%
where S0 represents the initial wound area at day 0 and St represents the wound area at time t.

14 days later, these mice were sacrificed, each wound tissue was removed and immersed in 4% paraformaldehyde solution for fixing. Then, the fixed wounds were made into paraffin sections for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and Masson’s trichrome staining, then the histological images were observed under optical microscopy.

Simplified reaction schemes for synthesis BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA were illustrated in Scheme 1. Firstly, the PCuRC were prepared via solvothermal method, and the QPCuRC was obtained after the modification of CAB-35. Then, the QPCuRC was combined with MSiO2 through electrostatic adsorption to synthesize QPCuRC@MSiO2. After that, BC was attracted to the surface of QPCuRC@MSiO2 through electrostatic interaction between the –NH3 +/N+ and HCO3 –. To prevent the early release of CO2 and enhance the PCE of the composite, the PDA was coated on the surface of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2 by self-polymerization of dopamine monomer under alkaline conditions, then the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA were successfully prepared which possess excellent photothermal effect, antibacterial performance, and super wound healing promotion property. The morphology of the synthesized NPs was examined via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and SEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a-d, the TEM images of QPCuRC, SiO2, QPCuRC@MSiO2, and BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA were displayed generally spherical with an average diameter of approximately 4.8 nm, 153 nm, 171 nm, and 182 nm, respectively. The original smooth of SiO2 became rough after combined with QPCuRC, which could be seen from the inset of Fig. 2 b-c. In addition, the hydrodynamic diameters of NPs were also measured by dynamic light scattering spectrometer (DLS). The average hydrodynamic diameters of QPCuRC, SiO2, QPCuRC@MSiO2, and BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA were 10.43 nm, 229.5 nm, 258.6 nm, and 283.2 nm, respectively (Fig. S1), which were consistent with TEM owing to DLS measured the overall hydrodynamic size. Fig. 1c revealed that the QPCuRC were distributed on the surface of MSiO2, suggested that the QPCuRC@MSiO2 were successfully prepared. The increase of diameter is attributed to the modification of BPEI and adsorption of QPCuRC. Further, the core–shell structure of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA showed a clear indication of PDA coating over BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2 (Fig. 1d). To further prove BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA was synthesized, the zeta potential of PCuRC (-36.3 eV), QPCuRC (-3.66 eV), QPCuRC@MSiO2 (15.8 eV), and BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA (-25.7 eV) were measured (Fig. 1j). The series changes of zeta potential proved the successful preparation of the nanocomposite. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of QPCuRC@MSiO2 and BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA were also determined (Fig. 1e), the typical peaks located at 23.69° and 24.04° could be attributed to the diffraction peaks of (002) facets of graphite, indicated that these nanoparticles consist of graphene-like structures. Additionally, the diffraction peak of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA accompanied by further broadening compared with QPCuRC@MSiO2, suggested that the amorphous character was increased, which could be assigned to the PDA coating.

The surface elemental compositions of the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As displayed in Fig. 1f, Si, S, C, N, O, and Cu elements could be observed from the XPS wide-scan spectra of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA, and the compositions were Si 5.46%, S 0.27%, C 63.49%, N 6.67%, O 23.89%, and Cu 0.22%, respectively. The peak at 103.8 eV was assigned to the Si2p (Fig. S2a). Fig. S2b shows the high-resolution C1s spectra of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA. The C1s peaks could be divided into four peaks, which were assigned to CO (288.5 eV), CS/CN (286 eV), CC/CC (284.95 eV), and C-Si (284.3 eV), respectively. Three peaks located at 400.56 eV, 399.65 eV, and 399 eV were ascribed to pyrrolic N, amino N, and pyridinic N in Fig. S2c, respectively. High-resolution O1s spectra (Fig. S2d) revealed the presence of COC (533.25 eV), C-OH (532.58 eV), and O-CO/N-CO (531.45 eV). In addition, the surface structure and composition of the samples were characterized by FTIR spectra. As shown in Fig. 1g, the spectra of QPCuRC, QPCuRC@MSiO2, and BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA exhibited absorption peaks at 3408 cm−1 owing to the stretching vibration of –OH and NH bonds, the characteristic peaks at 2924 and 2853 cm−1 could be attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of CH of the long methylene chain of CAB-35, and the bands at 1655 cm−1 could be assigned to the CO stretching vibration. The characteristic peaks of MSiO2, QPCuRC@MSiO2, and BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA at 1101 cm−1 could be attributed to the antisymmetric stretching vibration of Si-O-Si, the bands at 954 cm−1 could be ascribed to the Si-OH flexural vibration, and the absorption peaks at 801 cm−1 could be assigned to Si-O symmetrical stretching vibration. After being coated with PDA, the FTIR spectra of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA increased new absorption peaks at 1465 and 1397 cm−1 due to the presence of indole [58], suggested that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA were successfully prepared.

An excellent photothermal agent is anticipated to possess doughty absorbance in the NIR range of 750–850 nm, which is the lucency window for photothermal therapy [59]. As shown in Fig. 1h, compared with QPCuRC@MSiO2 and PDA, the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA exhibited more broader absorption in the NIR area, indicated that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA possess more superior photothermal effect than QPCuRC@MSiO2 and PDA. The excitation-independent photoluminescence (PL) behavior of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA was determined through exciting with different wavelengths from 340 nm to 440 nm (Fig. 1i). The BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA showed strong wavelength-dependent emission, and the maximum PL emission was about 722 nm under 360 nm wavelength excitation. Additionally, the emission obviously redshifted from 681 to 881 nm. Red fluorescence emission was favorable for biomedical applications because of the longer emission wavelengths could afford great potential to possess large tissue penetration depth [60,61]. Thereby, the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA could be regarded as an ideal candidate in the field of bioimaging and phototherapy agent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading of bicarbonate and CO2 release
ce:section.ce:para: To prove the loading of BC, the zeta potential was carried out. As displayed in Fig. 1j, the QPCuRC@MSiO2 exhibited an average zeta potential of 15.8 eV, while the zeta potential of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2 was about −2.3 eV (Fig. S3). The decreased surface zeta potential of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2 could be attributed to the combination of HCO3 – and –NH3 +/N+. Additionally, the release of CO2 at various concentrations of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA solution under different pH values was also investigated (Fig. S4). After being exposed to 808 nm laser for 10 min, the CO2 was obviously released in high concentrations (60, 120, and 240 µg/mL) owing to the instability of bicarbonate. In contrast, nearly no CO2 release was observed at low concentrations (15, 30 µg/mL) due to the release temperature of CO2 after irradiated was not achieved (＞42 ℃). In addition, the amount of released CO2 in acidic environment was much higher than neutral environment owing to the component of PDA would be decomposed under acidic conditions. Thus, the PDA plays a key role in the controlled release of CO2, furthermore, the component of PDA could also avoid the possibility of early release of CO2. These results proved that BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2 @PDA could be utilized as gas therapy agent to apply in biomedical field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal and photodynamic properties of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the NIR absorption, the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA could be regarded as an ideal photothermal agent. The photothermal performance of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA was evaluated under 808 nm NIR laser at various concentrations and power densities. The thermal images and the corresponding temperature changes of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA at different concentrations (from 15 µg/mL to 240 µg/mL) after irradiated with 808 nm laser (2 W/cm2) were shown in Fig. 2a-b, the pure water was considered as a control. Meanwhile, the thermal images of QPCuRC@MSiO2 at different concentrations were shown in Fig. S5. At a relatively high concentration (240 µg/mL), the temperature of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA solution increased quickly up to 52.9 ℃ after irradiation for 10 min, and the maximum temperature decreased with decreasing concentration. However, the temperature of QPCuRC@MSiO2 solution only increased up to 34.7 ℃ after treatment with 808 nm irradiation for 10 min. Fig. 2c displayed the temperature changes of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA solution (240 µg/mL) under different power densities (1.5, 2.0, and 2.5 W/cm2). The solution temperature gradually rises as the power densities increase. However, excessive power density is likely to cause certain damage to normal tissues. Hence, BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA concentration of 240 µg/mL and power density of 2.0 W/cm2 were chosen in the following experiments. Additionally, the photothermal stability of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA was also evaluated. As revealed in Fig. 2d, the photothermal profiles of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA were not significantly changes after repeated cycles of heating and cooling for 6 times, suggested that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA possess super photothermal stability and heat reproducibility. The photothermal conversion efficiency of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA was calculated to be 65.69% through the fitting parameters of the cooling curve (Fig. 2e), while the QPCuRC@MSiO2 was calculated to be 40.63% (Fig. S6). Compared with QPCuRC@MSiO2, the enhanced PCE of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA was mainly attributed to PDA coating, indicated that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA possess great potential to be a photothermal agent.

The photodynamic properties of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA were evaluated through the determination of ROS. The photogenerated single oxygen (1O2) is a major component of ROS which could induce irreversible damage to bacterial cells in PDT therapy. As shown in Fig. 3 a, no TEMP/1O2 signals were detected in non-irradiation group while the TEMP/1O2 signals were obviously detected in irradiation group, indicated that 1O2 was generated through energy transfer from the excited triplet state of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA to the ground state oxygen after irradiated (Fig. 3f). DPBF was used to monitor the generation of 1O2 which could quench DPBF and cause the apparent decrease of maximum absorbance at 410 nm. As displayed in Fig. 3b-c, the absorbance intensity of DPBF solution in the presence of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA alone has no changes, whereas the absorbance intensity of DPBF solution gradually decreased with the increased exposure time after being treated with BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA and 808 nm NIR laser, suggesting the photodynamic generation of 1O2. In addition, the generated ROS level in bacteria was also examined using DCFH-DA. The generated 1O2 could oxidize non-fluorescent DCFH-DA to 2′, 7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF) which possesses high fluorescence intensity, and the increase of the fluorescent signals reflect the generation level of ROS. As shown in Fig. 3d-e, nearly no fluorescent signals were monitored in deionized water. Compared with control group, the fluorescence intensity in bacteria was obviously enhanced after being treated with BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA, furthermore, stronger fluorescence peak was detected in the presence of 808 nm NIR laser. These results proved that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA possess photodynamic response of 1O2 generation under 808 nm laser irradiation, thereby, it could be regarded as a potential photodynamic agent for bacterial infection therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assay and cellular imaging of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the potential applications in biomedical field, the cytotoxicity and phototoxicity of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA were evaluated through Calcein-AM/PI co-staining and CCK-8 assay. Compared with Control and Control + L group, the L929 cells grow well with few dead cells (stained with red color) after being treated with BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA, and similarly after being irradiated with 808 nm NIR laser (Fig. 4 a), indicated that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA behave excellent biocompatibility. Fig. 4b presented the in vitro cell viability at different concentrations and irradiation conditions. Compared with BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA group, the cell viability of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA + L group was relatively low due to the generated hyperthermia and 1O2. However, the cell viability greater than 85% for all groups, even the samples concentration reached 240 µg/mL and irradiated for 10 min using 808 nm NIR laser, suggested that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA possess negligible phototoxicity which consistent with the result of Fig. 4a.

Due to the excellent biocompatibility and good fluorescence performance, the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA could also be utilized as bioimaging agent. MCF-7 cells were used for the in vitro imaging analysis via CLSM. As shown in Fig. 4c, blue, green, and red fluorescence were observed after excited with 405, 488, and 543 nm laser pulses, respectively, suggesting capabilities of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA as fluorescent probe for bioimaging. These results displayed that BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA have great potential in biomedical field.

The hemocompatibility evaluation of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA was performed using hemolysis assay, and the morphologies of red blood cells (RBCs) were recorded through microscope. As shown in Fig. 5 b-c, the optical images of RBCs exhibited negligible changes in morphology after being treated with normal saline solution and BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA. However, the RBCs were damaged after being treated with deionized water (Fig. 5a). Additionally, the supernatants were transparent and colorless after being treated with normal saline and different concentrations of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA solutions (15–240 µg/mL), while the supernatant treated with deionized water was bright red (Fig. S7). Accordingly, the hemolysis rate was also calculated after being incubated with different concentrations of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA. As revealed in Fig. 5d, all the hemolysis rates were less than 2%. The obtained results demonstrated that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA possess excellent hemocompatibility, thereby, it could be regarded as an ideal candidate in biological area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial activity of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the excellent properties of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA, the broad-spectrum antibacterial performance against E. coli and S. aureus was evaluated. Plate counting method was performed to evaluate the antibacterial property of QPCuRC@MSiO2 and BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA. In the presence or absence of 808 nm laser irradiation, sample solutions with different concentrations were investigated in all antibacterial assays. As displayed in Fig. 6 , the viability of E. coli and S. aureus was found to decrease with the increase of sample concentrations. Compared with non-irradiation groups, the growth of both bacteria was inhibited obviously, indicated that the generation of hyperthermia and ROS after 808 nm laser irradiation played a key role in antibacterial. Furthermore, the bacterial viability of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA + L group was lower than QPCuRC@MSiO2 + L group, which could be attributed to the modification of PDA. On the one hand, the PDA component has abundant catechol groups which could lead to number of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA adhered on the surface of bacteria; on the other hand, the PDA component possesses excellent photothermal effect, which could enhance the photothermal efficiency, thereby, the antibacterial effect was improved. As expected, up to 99.6% E. coli and 99.99% S. aureus were killed after being treated with BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA and 808 nm laser irradiation.

To explore the antibacterial mechanism, SEM imaging was utilized to investigate the morphology changes of E. coli and S. aureus. Both E. coli and S. aureus were treated with PBS, QPCuRC@MSiO2, and BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA, respectively, and all groups were divided into irradiation groups and non-irradiation groups. As shown in Fig. 7 a-b, the PBS groups of E. coli and S. aureus displayed smooth and intact cell membrane even under 808 nm irradiation, suggested that NIR laser alone showed no killing effect to bacteria. After treatment with QPCuRC@MSiO2, the negatively charged bacteria could adsorb the positively charged QPCuRC@MSiO2 by electrostatic force and then caused slight damage to the bacterial cell membrane, thus, some wrinkles were emerged in both bacteria. When 808 nm NIR laser was added, more severe damages were observed due to the generated hyperthermia and 1O2 caused protein denaturation and oxidative stress. In the group of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA + L, the morphologies of E. coli and S. aureus exhibited more serious damages compared with QPCuRC@MSiO2 + L group, confirmed that the modification of PDA improved the photothermal efficiency and adhesion of the antibacterial agent, thereby enhanced the antibacterial effect of the composite.

Additionally, the antibacterial performance evaluation of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA was further performed via live/dead bacterial cells staining assay. The viable bacteria were labeled with blue fluorescence by Hoechst 33,342 which could penetrate damaged and intact cell membranes. The dead bacteria were labeled with red fluorescence by PI which could only penetrate damaged cell membranes. As shown in Fig. 7c-d, the Control and Control + L groups emitted bright blue fluorescence, and nearly no red fluorescence was recorded, suggested that 808 nm laser alone negligibly affected the viability of bacteria. After being treated with QPCuRC@MSiO2 and BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA, significant red fluorescence signals were recorded, suggested that quaternary ammonium groups played a crucial role in antibacterial performance. In contrast, QPCuRC@MSiO2 treatment groups with NIR irradiation emitted an enhanced red fluorescence, indicated that the synergistic antibacterial effect was realized, including quaternary ammonium, PTT, and PDT. Notably, nearly no green spots were observed in BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA + L group, suggested that all bacteria were killed. The obtained results of live/dead bacterial cells staining assay exhibited the same antibacterial trend with plate counting method and SEM imaging, revealed that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA possess highly effective antibacterial activity under 808 nm laser irradiation.

Inspired by the excellent antibacterial efficiency and biocompatibility of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA in vitro, the in vivo therapeutic efficiency on open S. aureus-infected wounds was investigated, and the process of wound healing was recorded on 0, 1, 3, 7, 10, and 14 days, respectively (Fig. 8 a-d). The photothermal properties of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA were first evaluated. As displayed in Fig. 9 b, the temperature of the wound region, which was treated with BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA, was increased rapidly from 29.5 to 59.6 ℃ after irradiated with 808 nm NIR laser (2.0 W/cm2, 5 min), while the control group was increased to 39.2 ℃, confirmed that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA could convert the absorbed light into heat. Fig. 8b presented the optical images of wounds after different treatments. All groups showed a certain degree of infection in wound on day 1, the size of the wound gradually decreased and inflammatory response gradually slows down as time went on, indicated that the wound was healing gradually. On day 14, the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA + L group showed a complete wound closure, and the remained groups still possess mild inflammation. To visualize the changes of each wound area in the process of wound healing, wound trace figures were drawn by ImageJ and Power-Point software (Fig. 8c). Additionally, the wound healing rate was also calculated to quantitatively evaluate the therapeutic effect after being treated with different conditions. As revealed in Fig. 8d, the wound healing of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA + L group was significantly better than control group. Additionally, to confirm the release of CO2 could promote the wound healing process, the QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA + L group was also performed, and all the obtained results indicated that BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA have a much better wound healing effect under 808 nm laser irradiation. After 14 days of treatment, the wound healing rates of Control + L group, QPCuRC@MSiO2 + L group, QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA + L group, and BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA + L group were calculated to be 88%, 92%, 94%, and 100%, respectively, suggested that the release of CO2 could accelerate wound healing. The bacteria around the wound on day 14 were cultured on agar plates and counted to quantify the antibacterial and anti-infective performance of different groups (Fig. 8b). The number of bacterial colonies of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA + L group was the least, indicating the excellent antibacterial and anti-infective therapeutic effect. Notably, the locally released CO2 could reduce the inflammatory response and accelerate wound healing. These results confirmed that the synergistic antibacterial effect and wound healing promotion were realized via the integration of phototherapy and gas therapy. Taken together, the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA possess excellent synergistic antibacterial activity, anti-infective property, and wound healing promotion performance, thereby, it could be considered as a potential candidate to apply in biomedical field.

To further evaluate the therapeutic effect of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA, the wound tissues were collected for histological examination. H&E staining was used to evaluate the inflammatory reaction and cell proliferation. As shown in Fig. 9a, large number of tightly connected connective tissues, neoblood vessels, and mature hair follicles were observed, proved that the complete epidermal healing was achieved after being treated with BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA and 808 nm laser irradiation. Additionally, negligible inflammatory cells were observed, indicated that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA possess synergistic antibacterial property and wound healing promotion activity under 808 nm laser irradiation. On the contrary, obvious inflammatory cells were observed in PBS group, suggested that the epidermis was not fully healed. Less inflammatory cells were found in QPCuRC@MSiO2 + L and QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA + L groups, indicated that the epidermal healing speed was faster than PBS group and slower than BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA + L group. Notably, compared with QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA + L group, the enhanced wound healing performance of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA + L group could be attributed to the release of CO2, which could trigger more oxygen release from hemoglobin through Bohr effect and then effectively promote the healing of infected wounds. The formation and deposition of collagen in the wound were evaluated by Masson’s trichrome staining. As presented in Fig. 9a, mature collagen fibers and denser granulation tissue deposition were observed in the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA + L group, which were stained blue in full-thickness dermal wounds. Conversely, the remaining groups possess poorly developed shattered collagen bundles and looser granulation tissue deposition. The wound healing promotion behavior is probably attributed to the excellent broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and the controlled CO2 release of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA under 808 nm laser irradiation, decreasing the inflammation response and promoting the formation of connective tissue even new blood vessels in the infected wounds.

The in vivo biosafety of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA was assessed by the bodyweight of mice and H&E staining of key organs, including heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys. As shown in Fig. 8e, the bodyweight changes were consistent with Control + L group after being treated with different samples, indicated that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA possess negligible toxicity in vivo. Additionally, the histological analysis results presented that there was no inflammatory lesion compared with control group (Fig. 9c), suggested that the samples possess no long-term toxicity in vivo and could be considered as an ideal candidate in biomedical field.

In summary, a synergistic antibacterial nanoplatform has been successfully developed, furthermore, based on the reported literatures, this work integrated the phototherapy and gas therapy to accelerate the process of wound healing [31,62]. Taking advantage of the quaternary ammonium salt groups and the performance of the photothermal and photodynamic of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA, synergistic electrostatic attraction/PTT/PDT therapy could be realized after 808 nm laser irradiation (2.0 W/cm2, 10 min). The rough surfaces could trap bacteria, exhibiting high bacteria-binding performance and enhancing the antibacterial property [63]. In this work, the combination of SiO2 and QPCuRC enhanced the roughness of the composite, thus, the affinity between antibacterial agent and bacteria was improved so that the antibacterial activity of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA was higher than previous study [31]. Furthermore, the catechol groups of PDA could also enhance the adhesion performance [43,64], thus, the more effective antibacterial activity of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA was realized. Additionally, the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA realized the on-demand supply of CO2 due to the decomposition of PDA coating in acidic environment and the increase of temperature after irradiation. In situ release of CO2 could promote the wound healing process through the Bohr effect. Moreover, compared with other antimicrobial materials which based on silica carrier, the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA possess more extensive applications in biomedical field owing to the inherent luminescence [65,66]. In vitro and in vivo assays investigated that the BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA possess excellent biocompatibility, super antibacterial performance, and wound healing promotion by phototherapy and gas therapy. The current work affords a multifunctional nanoplatform for antibacterial, bioimaging, and wound healing promotion, which may possess practical significance in biomedical field. Further biodegradability study of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA is under way to confirm that it could be metabolized in the body without causing drug residues. Additionally, further work could also explore the antifouling performance of BC/QPCuRC@MSiO2@PDA.

Xiaohong Chu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yihan Liu: Formal analysis, Investigation. Pan Zhang: Formal analysis. Kaihang Li: Investigation. Wenli Feng: Visualization. Baohong Sun: Data curation. Ninglin Zhou: Conceptualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Jian Shen: Conceptualization, Project administration, Supervision.
